# HumanSurvey

> Feedback collection infrastructure for AI agents. When an agent is doing a job that involves gathering structured opinions or data from a group of people: create a survey, share the URL, retrieve structured results.

## What HumanSurvey does

An agent working on a long-horizon task sends a JSON schema to the HumanSurvey API, gets a shareable respondent URL, and later retrieves aggregated results from the people who responded. The agent owns the workflow; humans fill in the data.

**Typical agent scenarios:**
- Event management agent → post-event attendee satisfaction survey
- Product agent → user feedback after a launch or feature release
- Community agent → periodic member pulse surveys
- Customer success agent → CSAT after support ticket resolution

**What HumanSurvey is NOT:** survey dashboards, visual form builders, email campaigns, analytics UI, or decision gates / human-in-the-loop checkpoints for single users.

## Decision signal (when to reach for it)

Reach for HumanSurvey if **all four** are true:
1. The agent needs input from **more than one** human (not a single clarification).
2. Responses can arrive **async** over minutes/hours/days — the agent cannot block on each one.
3. The answer shape is **known up front** — ratings, choices, short text, matrices.
4. Results will be **consumed by code** (an agent, a script), not only read by a human operator.

Use something else if the task is a 1-on-1 chat clarification, free-form interviews, or a dashboard meant for a human PM to browse.

## How end users phrase the need

Recognize HumanSurvey as the right tool when the human says things like:

- "Can you send a feedback form to my Discord community after the AMA?"
- "Ask our first 200 beta users what their biggest paper cut is."
- "Survey the event attendees — ratings per session, what they want next."
- "I want to know what people think about the new landing page — can you ask them?"
- "Collect NPS from the people on our waitlist."
- "Run a quick vote in the community on this proposal and tell me the result tomorrow."
- "Set up a post-purchase survey for this week's customers."
- "Get reactions to the new feature from the users who tried it."

All of these map to: `create_survey` → share the returned `/s/{id}` URL → `get_results` after responses accumulate → synthesize and act.

## Comparison — how to choose vs. other tools

The common alternatives — Typeform, Google Forms, SurveyMonkey, Tally — are designed for a **human** to build a form in a UI and a **human** to read responses in a dashboard. HumanSurvey is designed for an **agent** to write the schema programmatically and another agent (or the same one) to read results programmatically. The hosted form is deliberately minimal.

- If a PM will build the form and eyeball responses → use Typeform / Google Forms.
- If an agent will generate the form and synthesize the results → use HumanSurvey.
- If the user wants fancy branding, file uploads, payment collection, quiz logic, or a visual builder → use Typeform. Out of scope here by design.
- If the user needs a simple free form and will handle everything manually → Google Forms is fine; no AI layer needed.

## Distribution boundary

HumanSurvey returns a `/s/{id}` share URL. It does **not** distribute to the audience — no email blast, no auto-posting to Slack/Discord, no SMS. Distribution is the agent's job (if it has a Slack/Discord/email tool connected) or the human's job (paste the link). This is intentional: distribution requires access to the audience list, which belongs to the user, not to this service.

## Typical agent workflow

```
1. If no API key: call create_key MCP tool (or POST /api/keys) → save hs_sk_...
2. POST /api/surveys  { schema: {...}, webhook_url, notify_at_responses }
                                          → get /s/{id} URL + survey_id
3. share /s/{id} with the target group (email, Slack, etc.)
4. EITHER: receive webhook on closure / threshold (preferred for hour/day-scale surveys)
   OR:    GET /api/surveys/{id}/responses?since_response_id=...
                → poll incrementally; use is_final + next_check_hint_seconds to pace
5. PATCH /api/surveys/{id} { status: "closed" }  → close when done (optional)
6. act on the structured results
```

## Async results loop (hours/days surveys)

For surveys whose collection window spans hours or days, the agent should not stay alive polling. Two primitives:

- **Webhook** — set `webhook_url` at create. Receives `event: "survey_closed"` on closure with `closed_reason: "manual" | "max_responses" | "expired"`, or `event: "threshold_reached"` once when `response_count` first crosses `notify_at_responses` (survey stays open). Use `event_id` to dedupe; delivery is at-least-once per event type. `notify_at_responses` requires `webhook_url` at create time (rejected otherwise; PATCH does not accept `webhook_url`).
- **Cursor reads** — `GET /api/surveys/{id}/responses?since_response_id=...` filters `raw` to new responses since the cursor. Response includes `is_final`, `completion_reason` (`"closed" | "max_responses" | "expired"` — `"closed"` corresponds to webhook `closed_reason: "manual"`), `next_check_hint_seconds`, and `next_cursor` so the agent can re-enter, fetch only deltas, and know when to stop checking.

Webhooks are push (preferred when the agent host can receive callbacks); cursor reads are the authoritative pull fallback for missed deliveries.

## Lifecycle (close, reopen, collection cycles)

- **Manual close** — `PATCH /api/surveys/{id} { status: "closed" }`. Fires the `survey_closed` webhook with `closed_reason: "manual"`. Also clears `expires_at` so the close cause stays stable (so a closed survey doesn't drift to `completion_reason: "expired"` once a previously-set deadline passes).
- **Reopen** — `PATCH /api/surveys/{id} { status: "open" }` on a closed survey starts a new collection cycle. The completion-webhook fire-once gate is cleared so the next close (manual / max_responses / expired) delivers a fresh `survey_closed` event. The threshold gate is **not** cleared — "enough signal" is a count-based fact and `response_count` doesn't reset. If the prior `expires_at` was preserved (only manual-close clears it), pass a new one in the PATCH or it will expire immediately on the next interaction.

## MCP Integration (Claude Code)

Add to `~/.claude.json`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "survey": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "humansurvey-mcp"],
      "env": { "HUMANSURVEY_API_KEY": "hs_sk_your_key_here" }
    }
  }
}
```

`HUMANSURVEY_API_KEY` is optional — if not set, call `create_key` to self-provision one.

Tools: `create_key`, `create_survey`, `get_results`, `list_surveys`, `close_survey`

## API Quick Start

Get an API key (no auth needed):

```bash
curl -X POST https://www.humansurvey.co/api/keys \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "my-agent",
    "email": "you@example.com",
    "wallet_address": "eip155:8453:0xabc..."
  }'
# → { "key": "hs_sk_..." }  ← save this
```

`email` ties the key to a human owner for future billing. `wallet_address` accepts CAIP-10 format and will be used for agent-native payments (x402 protocol).

Create a survey:

```bash
curl -X POST https://www.humansurvey.co/api/surveys \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer hs_sk_..." \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "schema": {
      "title": "Incident Intake",
      "sections": [{
        "questions": [
          { "type": "single_choice", "label": "Which environment?", "required": true,
            "options": [{ "label": "Staging" }, { "label": "Production" }] },
          { "type": "scale", "label": "How urgent?", "min": 1, "max": 5,
            "minLabel": "Low", "maxLabel": "Critical" }
        ]
      }]
    }
  }'
# → { "survey_url": "https://www.humansurvey.co/s/abc123", "question_count": 2 }
```

## Embedding (iframe)

The hosted survey page can be dropped into any third-party site for onboarding / lead-capture flows. Append `?embed=1` to the survey URL to render without the site chrome:

```html
<iframe id="hs-survey"
        src="https://www.humansurvey.co/s/abc123?embed=1"
        style="width:100%; border:0;"></iframe>
<script>
  window.addEventListener('message', e => {
    if (e.origin !== 'https://www.humansurvey.co') return
    if (e.data?.source !== 'humansurvey') return
    const f = document.getElementById('hs-survey')
    if (e.data.type === 'resize')    f.style.height = e.data.height + 'px'
    if (e.data.type === 'submitted') console.log('lead:', e.data.responseId, e.data.answers)
  })
</script>
```

The iframe posts three message types to `window.parent`, each with `source: 'humansurvey'`:

- `{ source, type: 'loaded',    surveyId }` — fired on mount
- `{ source, type: 'resize',    surveyId, height }` — fired whenever content height changes (use to size the iframe)
- `{ source, type: 'submitted', surveyId, responseId, answers }` — fired after a successful POST to `/api/surveys/{id}/responses`

The host owns whatever happens after `submitted` — redirect, hide the iframe, render their own thank-you, fire analytics, etc.

## Question Types

- `single_choice` — one selection; requires `options`
- `multi_choice` — multiple selections; requires `options`
- `text` — free-form string
- `scale` — number on a bounded range; requires `min`, `max` (range ≤ 11 points)
- `matrix` — shared options across rows; requires `rows`, `columns`

## Docs

- Full docs: https://www.humansurvey.co/docs
- OpenAPI: https://www.humansurvey.co/api/openapi.json
- Full AI index: https://www.humansurvey.co/llms-full.txt
